articles.title: Tether Breakage Across First-generation and Second-generation Vertebral Body Tethering Devices in Treating Adolescent Idiopathic Scoliosis.
articles.authors: Coleman TA, Kim A, Desai V, Parent S, Miyanji F, Alanay A, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective analysis of prospectively collected multicenter registry
articles.population: Patients with adolescent idiopathic scoliosis treated with thoracic vertebral body tethering
articles.sample_size: 231 patients across 12 centres (179 first-generation, 52 second-generation implants)
articles.outcomes: Radiographic tether breakage rate, revision surgery rate, conversion to posterior spinal fusion within 2 years
articles.take_home: Tether breakage was found in about half of all patients, occurring at similar rates in both older and newer tethering devices (52% versus 44%), despite surgeons reporting fewer breakages with the newer devices. Revision surgery and conversion to spinal fusion rates were low and comparable between both device generations.

articles.title: Radiographic Measurement of Lower Limb Length Discrepancy: A Comparison of Three Measurement Techniques for Assessing Total and Segmental Limb Lengths.
articles.authors: Kadhim M, Kelly N, Sabharwal S
articles.population: Skeletally immature patients with limb length discrepancy scheduled for epiphysiodesis
articles.outcomes: Differences in total and segmental limb length measurements between three radiographic measurement methods; agreement analysis using Bland-Altman plots
articles.take_home: Three different radiographic methods for measuring limb length differences produced very similar results (differences of less than 2 mm), but doctors should use the same method consistently as small variations can be affected by leg angular deformities.

articles.title: Animal-Assisted Therapy Reduces Patient Reported Pain During Office Pin Removal Following Supracondylar Humerus Fracture Fixation: A Randomized Controlled Study.
articles.authors: Arif HA, Feijoo E, Padgett M, Nishikawa A, Williams KA, Strom SF, et al.
articles.population: Children aged 3 to 10 years undergoing pin removal after supracondylar humerus fracture fixation
articles.sample_size: 52 patients (27 in dog group, 25 in control group)
articles.outcomes: Patient-reported pain using Wong-Baker FACES Pain Rating Scale, behavioural distress using FLACC scale, heart rate measurements, and parental-reported anxiety
articles.take_home: Children who had a certified therapy dog present during pin removal reported significantly lower pain and showed less behavioural distress than those without a dog, with lower heart rates during the procedure. Animal-assisted therapy may help reduce pain and anxiety during this common outpatient orthopaedic procedure.

articles.title: Treatment and outcomes of fractures in children: a registry-based cohort study of 10,144 fractures.
articles.authors: Laaksonen T, Grahn P, Ahonen M, Ahola JA, Helenius I, Nietosvaara Y
articles.population: Children with fractures treated at a Level I trauma centre
articles.outcomes: Surgical timing, treatment method, complication rates, unplanned return to surgery, permanent nerve injuries, deep infections
articles.take_home: Children's fracture treatment was safe and effective, with most operations starting promptly and permanent nerve injuries or serious infections being very rare. Most forearm fractures were successfully managed without general anaesthetic in the emergency department.

articles.title: Postpartum haemorrhage: epidemiology, consequences, and missed opportunities.
articles.authors: Coomarasamy A, Sindhu KN, Gallos I, Periyathambi N, Price MJ, Yunas I, et al.
articles.population: Women giving birth globally
articles.sample_size: Estimated 27 million women with PPH annually worldwide
articles.outcomes: PPH prevalence, mortality, morbidity, economic burden, and risk factors
articles.take_home: Postpartum haemorrhage affects millions of women each year and remains a leading cause of maternal death. The WHO has redefined PPH to enable earlier diagnosis and treatment, with recommended prevention strategies including uterotonic drugs for all births and additional measures for high-risk women.

articles.title: Prevention of postpartum haemorrhage: from evidence to implementation at scale.
articles.authors: Gallos ID, Sindhu KN, Yunas I, Sobhy S, Mavrides E, Sheikh L, et al.
articles.study_type: Systematic review and Cochrane network meta-analysis
articles.population: Women giving birth
articles.outcomes: Effectiveness of different uterotonic drugs for preventing postpartum haemorrhage; side-effect profiles
articles.take_home: Oxytocin or carbetocin given at birth are the safest and most effective single drugs to prevent postpartum haemorrhage, with minimal side-effects. Preventing postpartum haemorrhage also requires addressing anaemia, managing risk factors like high BMI and gestational diabetes, and ensuring women give birth in well-resourced hospitals.

articles.title: Reliability and clinical utility of paediatric ankle fracture classifications : a systematic review.
articles.authors: Teo LS, Xiao L, Yeo BKK, Rayapati R, Lim AKS, Hoipo Hui J, et al.
articles.population: Paediatric ankle fractures
articles.outcomes: Inter- and intraobserver reliability of classification systems; clinical utility in guiding clinical decisions and outcomes; ability to predict premature physeal closure and angular deformity
articles.take_home: The Dias-Tachdjian classification system is the most reliable for doctors to use consistently when classifying children's ankle fractures, whilst the Salter-Harris system is most commonly used and best predicts long-term complications like premature closure of the growth plate. Using both classification types together may help doctors better predict outcomes and plan treatment.

articles.title: The impact of Chile's multipronged food labelling and advertising law on early childhood excess weight: a cohort difference-in-differences study.
articles.authors: Paraje G, Valdes N, Macaya AV, Corvalan C, Popkin B
articles.population: School children aged 4-6 years in Chilean public and publicly subsidised schools
articles.outcomes: Probability of excess weight (overweight or obesity) measured by BMI
articles.take_home: Chile's food labelling and advertising law, which included warning labels and marketing restrictions, led to measurable reductions in excess weight among young children, with girls and boys showing 2-3% lower probability of excess weight after 18 months of exposure. The findings suggest that comprehensive food environment policies can help reduce childhood obesity.

articles.title: Low concentration atropine eye drops and progression of myopia in children: multicentre placebo controlled, double masked, randomised trial in the UK (CHAMP-UK).
articles.authors: Azuara-Blanco A, Logan NS, McConnell E, Kearney S, Kirk G, Jones S, et al.
articles.study_type: Multicentre, double masked, randomised controlled trial
articles.population: Children aged 6-12 years with myopia between -0.50 and -10.0 dioptres
articles.sample_size: 289 children (192 atropine, 97 placebo); 230 completed with primary outcome data
articles.outcomes: Primary: spherical equivalent refractive error after two years. Secondary: axial length change, visual acuity, reading speed, pupil diameter, adverse events, quality of life, tolerability
articles.take_home: Low concentration atropine (0.01%) eye drops significantly slowed myopia progression in children compared to placebo, reducing the change in eye refraction by 0.33 dioptres over two years. The treatment was well tolerated with no serious adverse events related to the drops.

articles.title: Exa-cel in Children with Transfusion-Dependent beta-Thalassemia or Sickle Cell Disease.
articles.authors: Frangoul H, de la Fuente J, Chopra Y, Meisel R, Amrolia PJ, Algeri M, et al.
articles.population: Children aged 5 to 11 years with transfusion-dependent beta-thalassaemia or sickle cell disease
articles.sample_size: 15 children with beta-thalassaemia and 11 with sickle cell disease
articles.outcomes: Transfusion independence for at least 12 consecutive months in beta-thalassaemia; freedom from severe vaso-occlusive crises for at least 12 consecutive months in sickle cell disease
articles.take_home: Of children followed for at least 16 months, all those with beta-thalassaemia became independent from blood transfusions and all those with sickle cell disease stopped having severe pain crises. However, all children experienced serious side effects, and one child with beta-thalassaemia died from liver damage related to the chemotherapy preparation.

articles.title: Interleukin-10 Autoantibodies and HLA-DRB1*01:03 in Inflammatory Bowel Disease.
articles.authors: Gharahdaghi N, Yeh PJ, Ceron-Gutierrez L, Griffin H, Gordon H, Jayamanne C, et al.
articles.study_type: Case-control study with biomarker and genetic analysis
articles.population: Patients with inflammatory bowel disease and controls without IBD
articles.outcomes: Presence of interleukin-10 neutralising autoantibodies and association with HLA-DRB1*01:03 genetic variant
articles.take_home: About 3.5% of IBD patients had autoantibodies that block interleukin-10, a protein that normally reduces inflammation, and these patients were much more likely to carry a specific genetic variant (HLA-DRB1*01:03). This finding may help explain why some IBD patients have more severe disease and could potentially guide future treatments.

articles.title: Advances in supporting development in autistic children and youth.
articles.authors: Penner M, Zwaigenbaum L, Piroddi N, Park S, Minhas RS, Singal D
articles.population: Autistic children and youth
articles.outcomes: Developmental and adaptive skills, co-occurring conditions, autonomy, community participation, intervention safety and effectiveness
articles.take_home: Research shows that supporting development in autistic children from early years onwards can help improve outcomes, but interventions must be safe and align with what autistic people themselves identify as important. Autistic people emphasise that acceptance and genuine inclusion in communities matter most for having a good life.

articles.title: Time is of the Essence! Long-Term Functional, Motor and Sensory Outcomes After Surgical Fixation of Pediatric Medial Epicondyle Fractures: A Retrospective Cohort Study.
articles.authors: Akin AE, Erkan S, Okcu G
articles.population: Children under 18 years old who underwent surgical fixation for medial epicondyle fracture
articles.sample_size: 22 patients with complete long-term follow-up (identified from 40 eligible patients)
articles.outcomes: Functional scores (QuickDASH, pain), range of motion, motor strength (grip and pinch), sensation, stability, complications
articles.take_home: Children who had surgery for medial epicondyle fractures achieved excellent long-term results with good strength, sensation and stability, but those who waited longer before surgery had some permanent loss of elbow movement range. Early surgery combined with rehabilitation offers the best outcomes.

articles.title: Complications of Implant Removal of Pediatric Femoral Shaft Fractures.
articles.authors: Aslanturk O, Roberts C, Siddique R, Rowland J, Sheffer B, Kelly DM
articles.population: Paediatric patients with diaphyseal femur fractures treated surgically
articles.outcomes: Operative time, estimated blood loss, length of stay, wound complications, infection, hematoma, bony overgrowth, refracture
articles.take_home: Implant removal after paediatric femur fractures is generally safe with low complication rates. Plate removal took longer and caused more bleeding than nail removal, whilst nail removal was associated with more bony overgrowth and longer times between initial fixation and removal.

articles.title: Advances in the management of ADHD in children and adolescents.
articles.authors: Robaey P, Rogers MA, Schachar RJ
articles.population: Children and adolescents with ADHD
articles.outcomes: Diagnostic accuracy, effectiveness of behavioural interventions, psychosocial treatments, lifestyle changes, cognitive training, neurofeedback, and pharmacological treatments
articles.take_home: ADHD requires long-term, individualised care combining several approaches. Behavioural parent training, school-based interventions and talking therapies help a little, whilst medication (particularly stimulants) remains the most effective treatment, with newer approaches allowing more personalised prescribing.

articles.title: Factors associated with open reduction of pediatric type III supracondylar humerus fractures.
articles.authors: Najd Mazhar F, Zareie B, Shariatzadeh H, Hasan A, Jafari Kafiabadi M, Akhlaghi N, et al.
articles.population: Children aged 3-13 years with Gartland type III supracondylar humerus fractures
articles.outcomes: Factors associated with need for open reduction versus successful closed reduction and pin fixation
articles.take_home: Older children, those with flexion-type fractures, and those with fractures in the lower part of the bone were more likely to need open surgery rather than closed reduction. Low-level fractures create smaller, more cartilage-based bone fragments that are harder to keep stable with closed reduction alone.

articles.title: A Comparison of the Effectiveness of Different Fixation Strategies in Pediatric and Adolescent Subtrochanteric Femur Fractures.
articles.authors: Munshi MA, Siddabattula R, Najera Saltos D, Pereira D, Cornaghie M, Hosseinzadeh P
articles.population: Children and adolescents aged 18 years or under treated operatively for subtrochanteric femur fractures
articles.outcomes: Radiographic healing, malalignment rates, pain and mobility scores (PROMIS), complications
articles.take_home: Three different surgical fixation methods (flexible nails, rigid nails, and plates) all resulted in successful bone healing and functional improvement in children with subtrochanteric femur fractures. Although flexible nails showed more early misalignment at 6 weeks, this difference was not significant by 3 months, and all methods were considered viable options.

articles.title: Prospective Methodology Results in Higher Rates of Reported Complications Following Pediatric ACL Reconstruction.
articles.authors: Martin H, Ellis H, Agonias K, Van Pelt R, Saper M, Niu EL, et al.
articles.population: Children and young people aged 18 years or under undergoing anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction
articles.sample_size: 8516 cases in the prospective SCORE database (2018-2025)
articles.outcomes: Complication rates within 8 months after surgery, including stiffness, reoperation, infection, wound-healing delay, pain, nerve damage, readmission, and blood clots
articles.take_home: About 1 in 9 children had a complication within 8 months of knee ligament surgery, most commonly stiffness. Prospective tracking found higher rates of some complications like reoperation and pain compared to historical records, suggesting careful monitoring picks up more problems that might otherwise be missed.

articles.title: A Comparison of Patient-Reported and Radiographic Outcomes Across Rigid and Flexible Flatfeet Following Calcaneal Lengthening Osteotomy in Pediatric Patients.
articles.authors: Munshi MA, Siddabattula R, Pereira D, Tang N, Hosseinzadeh P
articles.population: Children aged 8 to 18 years with painful flatfeet (rigid or flexible) undergoing calcaneal lengthening osteotomy
articles.sample_size: 72 children (114 feet) with minimum 6 months follow-up
articles.outcomes: Patient-reported pain and mobility scores (PROMIS); radiographic measurements including talo-first metatarsal angle, talonavicular uncoverage, incongruency angle, calcaneal pitch, and medial cuneiform-5th metatarsal height
articles.take_home: Calcaneal lengthening osteotomy significantly improved pain and radiographic measurements in children with both flexible and rigid flatfeet, with comparable results between the two groups. The procedure showed promising short-term outcomes for treating painful flatfeet that have not responded to conservative treatment.

articles.title: Limb occlusion pressure adjusted tourniquet use results in adequate hemostasis in pediatric limb surgeries: a prospective clinical study.
articles.authors: Jain S, Gupta S, Raghav L, Kumar N, Kumar Y, Kalia P
articles.population: Children aged 1-15 years undergoing upper or lower limb surgery
articles.outcomes: Adequate hemostasis achieved in 95.55% of procedures; no tourniquet-related complications observed
articles.take_home: Using a handheld Doppler to measure limb occlusion pressure and set tourniquet pressure accordingly works well and safely in children having limb surgery, allowing lower pressures to be used compared to standard adult practice.

articles.title: Surgical pinning after loss of reduction in Salter-Harris type I-II distal radius fractures does not improve outcomes: A propensity matched cohort study.
articles.authors: Oral M, Kolac UC, Aydinoglu HS, Ibik S, Morkan M, Sili MV, et al.
articles.population: Children under 16 years old with Salter-Harris types I and II distal radius fractures who lost reduction after initial closed reduction
articles.outcomes: Mayo wrist score, DASH score, PRWE score, and radiographic parameters (radial height, dorsal angulation, dorsal translation, radial inclination, physeal alignment)
articles.take_home: In children with these fracture types who lost reduction, continuing non-surgical treatment worked just as well as surgery, with no meaningful differences in wrist function or X-ray appearance at final follow-up. This suggests surgery may not be needed routinely for these fractures in growing children.
